dialogue.utterance: Dispatch to Unit 3 for chest pain at 1234 Columbian Way, Richmond.
Hello ma'am, I'm Erica with Medical Rescue, and this is my partner, Ben. What made you call 911 today?
I'm having chest pain and some tenderness in my left arm.
Can you tell me your name?
My name is Mary.
What county are we in, Mary?
Richmond, Virginia.
What date is today, Mary?
Uh, I think it's the 25th.
And what happened today that made you call 911?
It's some chest and arm pain. Started this morning.
When did the pain start exactly?
Probably around 6:30 a.m., but it started getting worse about half an hour ago.
When you touch that area, does the pain get worse, Mary?
She says it gets worse when she moves her arm.
When did this sensation begin, back to the first time you noticed it?
Probably three days ago. It got really bad after I started a new medication.
What makes the pain better or worse?
It's better when I lie down. Worse when I move or push on it.
On a scale from 0 to 10, with 10 being the worst pain you've ever had, how bad would you describe your pain now, Mary?
Four. Very sharp.
Can you take a deep breath in for me?
Breath sounds are equal, bilateral, clear.
Pulse is fast, around 139.
Blood pressure is 138 over 80. Radial pulse is strong.
BP is 138 over 80.
Respirations are 20 per minute, even.
O2 saturation is 100 percent.
Heart rate is 139.
Skin is warm, pink, and dry. No signs of swelling, edema, or pallor. No bleeding noted.
Based on your report and findings, we're considering anxiety disorder as a potential cause due to the recent medication change.
We are also considering non-cardiac chest pain as a differential diagnosis, as the pain is reproducible with palpation and movement.
I have no drug allergies.
Are there any medications you are taking, and how long have you been taking them?
She is on new hepatitis C medication - started about a week ago.
What are the names of those medications?
I don't know the names, but she said she took four baby aspirin before we called 911.
Do you have any past medical issues, Mary?
I was shot in the stomach a long time ago. Hepatitis C, too.
Are you feeling more anxious since starting the new medication?
Yes, she says she's been feeling more anxious since starting the new medication.
After your breath, I'd like to check your chest and back for tenderness.
Palpation reveals localized tenderness in the left axillary area, which radiates slightly to the left bicep. Pain is reproducible with palpation and movement.
We performed a glucose check. The result is 0 mg/dL.
We've set up the 4-lead monitor and are currently evaluating the ECG.
We performed a 12-lead ECG as per protocol. The reading shows no ST elevation or dysrhythmias.
Your EKG and vitals are negative for acute issues.
Mary, based on today's findings and our discussion, this seems like a non-urgent chest pain. Are you refusing any further transport to the hospital?
Yes, I do not want to be transported.
As per Richmond PD and your friend John here, you are not in distress and seem appropriate for at-home monitoring.
If anything changes, you can call 911 again for reassessment. Do you accept these conditions?
Yes, I understand and I'll do that. I'll just call my doctor tomorrow.
We will need you to sign a refusal form so you are clear on the risks. Mary, do you consent to document refusal of transport to the ER?
Signature paper is printed. Mary, did you understand the risks of choosing not to go to the hospital?
Yes.
We'll log your refusal in the ePCR and clear out of the scene. Thank you for your time, Mary.
Patient is 55 YOF, A&Ox4, 138 over 80, HR 139, 20 RR, ox 100%, pain 4/10 at the left axillary area, radiating. Negative for ST elevation or cardiac compromise. No major bleeding or signs of pallor.
We're going to log the refusal and clear out of the scene. Thank you for your time, Mary, and please reach out to your PCP tomorrow.
Per procedure, we'll notify dispatch and add notes on EPCR. M172 is returning to service.
